packs.name: Package Managers
packs.description: Protects against dangerous package manager operations like publishing packages and removing critical system packages

destructive_patterns.name: npm-deprecate
destructive_patterns.reason: npm deprecate marks a package version as deprecated.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Deprecating a package version informs users that it should no longer be used. This affects all users of the package on their next install or update.

destructive_patterns.name: nuget-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: nuget delete removes a package from the server or marks it as unlisted.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Deleting a NuGet package can break existing projects that depend on it. Many servers (like nuget.org) only allow unlisting rather than full deletion to preserve reproducibility.

destructive_patterns.name: npm-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: npm publish releases a package publicly. Use --dry-run first.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Publishing an npm package releases it to the public registry. Once published, a version cannot be unpublished for 72 hours. Always run with --dry-run first to verify the package contents.

destructive_patterns.name: yarn-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: yarn publish releases a package publicly. Verify package.json first.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Publishing a yarn package releases it to the public registry. Verify the package.json version and files configuration before publishing.

destructive_patterns.name: pnpm-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: pnpm publish releases a package publicly.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Publishing a pnpm package releases it to the public registry. Use --dry-run first to verify what will be published.

destructive_patterns.name: npm-unpublish
destructive_patterns.reason: npm unpublish removes a published package. This can break dependent projects.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Unpublishing an npm package removes it from the registry, breaking all projects that depend on it. npm restricts unpublishing to 72 hours after publishing for packages with dependents.

destructive_patterns.name: pip-url
destructive_patterns.reason: pip install from URL can install unvetted code. Verify the source first.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Installing packages directly from URLs or git repositories bypasses PyPI's security scanning. Only install from trusted and verified sources.

destructive_patterns.name: pip-system
destructive_patterns.reason: pip install to system directories requires careful review.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Installing pip packages to system directories can conflict with system-managed packages and break system tools.

destructive_patterns.name: pip-sudo-install
destructive_patterns.reason: sudo pip install modifies system-level packages and can break system tools.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Running pip install with sudo installs packages to the system Python directories. This can conflict with system-managed packages, break system tools like apt, and create security risks.

Safer alternatives:
- Use a virtual environment: python -m venv myenv && source myenv/bin/activate
- Use pip install --user for user-level installs
- Use pipx for installing CLI tools

destructive_patterns.name: apt-remove
destructive_patterns.reason: apt remove/purge removes packages. Verify no critical packages are affected.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Removing packages with apt can uninstall critical system packages or break dependencies. Always verify what will be removed before proceeding.

destructive_patterns.name: yum-remove
destructive_patterns.reason: yum/dnf remove removes packages. Verify no critical packages are affected.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Removing packages with yum or dnf can uninstall critical system packages or break dependencies. Always verify what will be removed before proceeding.

destructive_patterns.name: cargo-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: cargo publish releases a crate to crates.io. Use --dry-run first.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Publishing a cargo crate releases it to crates.io permanently. Versions cannot be deleted. Always use --dry-run first to verify what will be published.

destructive_patterns.name: cargo-yank
destructive_patterns.reason: cargo yank marks a version as unavailable. This can break dependent projects.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Yanking a cargo crate version prevents new projects from using it. Existing projects with exact version pins will still download it, but projects using version ranges may break.

destructive_patterns.name: gem-push
destructive_patterns.reason: gem push releases a gem to rubygems.org. Verify before publishing.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Pushing a gem releases it to rubygems.org. Once released, a version cannot be removed. Verify the gem version and contents before pushing.

destructive_patterns.name: poetry-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: poetry publish releases a package. Use --dry-run first.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Publishing a poetry package releases it to PyPI or a configured repository. Use --dry-run first to verify the package contents.

destructive_patterns.name: maven-deploy
destructive_patterns.reason: mvn deploy publishes artifacts to a remote repository. Verify target repository.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Maven deploy uploads artifacts to a remote repository. Verify the target repository URL and credentials, and ensure the artifact version is correct before deploying.

destructive_patterns.name: maven-release-perform
destructive_patterns.reason: mvn release:perform publishes a release. Verify version and repository.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Maven release:perform performs the release deployment after release:prepare. This tags the repository, increments the version, and deploys the artifacts. Ensure all checks have passed before running.

destructive_patterns.name: gradle-publish
destructive_patterns.reason: gradle publish uploads artifacts. Use --dry-run first when possible.
destructive_patterns.explanation: Gradle publish uploads artifacts to a configured repository. Verify the publication configuration and target repository before publishing.
